1: Joshua's Hebrew name (Yehoshua) means 'YHWH saves' — the same name as Jesus (Greek Iēsous, from the LXX transliteration of Yehoshua). The name-identity is not coincidental: Hebrews 4:8 uses the identity of names to make a theological argument ('if Joshua [Iēsous] had given them rest ...'). Joshua is Israel's deliverer who leads the people into the promised land after Moses; Jesus is the deliverer who leads his people into the ultimate rest that Canaan only shadowed. The structural parallel is complete: Moses (law) could not bring Israel into rest; Joshua/Jesus (the one YHWH saves) is the one who completes the journey.
2: Moshe avdi met — "Moses my servant is dead." The title eved (servant) is not withdrawn at Moses's death; he is still "my servant" even as his death is announced. The perfect met (he died) states a completed fact. The transition is abrupt: YHWH does not pause to grieve before giving Joshua his commission. The brevity models how covenant purpose continues uninterrupted through the death of individuals — a pattern the NT applies to Christ's risen commissioning of the disciples immediately after the resurrection.
3: kol makom asher tidrok kaph reglekhem bo natati lakhem — "every place where the sole of your foot treads I have given to you." The perfect natati (I have given) describes a future gift as already accomplished — the Hebrew prophetic perfect. In God's decree the land is already given before the foot treads; the conquest is the temporal enactment of an eternal decision. The foot-treading idiom (possession by physical presence) is ancient Near Eastern legal language for land-grant confirmation.
4: The boundaries — wilderness, Lebanon, Euphrates, Hittite land, Great Sea — define the maximal extent of the Abrahamic land promise (cf. Gen 15:18-21: from the River of Egypt to the Euphrates). The fourfold boundary formula is a formal legal description, not geographic hyperbole. The actual settlement of the judges period fell far short of these limits; the promise's full scope remains eschatological in the NT reading (Rom 4:13: heir of the world).
5: lo yityatsev ish lefanekha — "no man shall take a stand before you." Nitsav (hitpael of ytsb) means to station oneself, take a firm stand in combat. The negated promise reverses this: no enemy shall successfully hold position against Joshua. The grounds follow: ki asher hayiti im Moshe ehyeh imakh — "as I was with Moses, ehyeh (I will be) with you." The verb ehyeh is the first-person form of the divine name YHWH (I AM / I WILL BE, Exod 3:14) — the promise of divine presence carries the weight of the divine name.
6: hazaq we'emats — "be strong and courageous." This two-word imperative is the key command of Joshua 1, appearing three times (vv. 6, 7, 9) plus a fourth in v.18 from the people. Hazaq (from hzq) is physical-moral strength; amats (from 'mts) is resolution, determination that does not waver. Neither is an emotion to manufacture — they are commanded responses to a stated ground: YHWH's presence and faithfulness to the promise. The inheritance is given; courage means acting on what God has already secured.
7: raq hazaq we'emats me'od lishmos la'asot kekol hatorah — "only be very strong and courageous to observe to do according to all the law." Raq introduces a qualification or restriction: the one caveat on the promise is covenant faithfulness. lo tasur mimenu yamin usmol — "do not turn from it right or left" — the idiom for complete, bilateral fidelity (Deut 5:32; 17:11). Obedience is not the condition of salvation but the structure of covenant life in the land.
8: lo yamush sefer hatorah hazeh mipicha — "this Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth." Mush = to depart, remove; the negative command pictures Torah as a permanent resident on the lips — the orality of the ancient scroll tradition. vehagita bo yomam valailah — hagah (to meditate/murmur) is an oral word: it describes the low vocalization of one reviewing a text, the same term used in Ps 1:2. The Law is not a file to consult but a text to speak continuously. ki az tatsliyach et derakekha — then you will make your way prosperous; tsalah (hiphil) = cause to succeed/prosper — covenant success attached to Torah-immersion.
9: The third repetition of hazaq we'emats is paired with the strongest ground: ki imkha YHWH elohekha bekhol asher telekh — "for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go." The ki (for/because) makes divine presence the logical basis of courage, not a reward for it. The phrase bekhol asher telekh (wherever you go) is unlimited — not only in battle but in every domain of life. The NT applies this same principle to Christian confidence through Christ's permanent presence (Matt 28:20; Heb 13:5, quoting Josh 1:5).
10: vayyetsav Yehoshua et shoterei ha'am — "Joshua commanded the officers of the people." Shotrim (officers/scribes) were the administrative tier of Israelite governance, responsible for organizing and communicating commands to the community. The smooth handover from Moses to Joshua is institutional as well as personal — Joshua inherits both the prophetic and administrative roles.
11: ivru beqerev hamachaneh — "pass through the midst of the camp." The military camp of Israel in the wilderness period had a defined organization (Num 2). The three-day notice before the Jordan crossing (od shloshet yamim) creates space for preparation and communal readiness. Tsiydah = provisions/supplies for the journey — the same word used in the Passover preparation language.
12: Joshua's address to the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh recalls their land grant east of the Jordan (Num 32). Their situation is legally complex: they have their inheritance already but are obligated to help their brothers take theirs. The address tests whether tribal self-interest will override covenant solidarity.
13: zakhor et hadavar asher tsivah etkhem Moshe eved YHWH — "remember the word that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you." Zakhor (remember) in Hebrew means more than mental recall — it means to act in accordance with what is remembered. Menuchah (rest) here is used of the Transjordanian settlement: YHWH has given them rest. The same term becomes the key word in Hebrews 3-4 for the eschatological rest Christ provides.
14: nashekhem tap umiknekhem — "your wives, your little ones, and your livestock." The listing distinguishes those who stay from those who must fight. gibborei hachayil — "the mighty men of valor" — the military elite; chayil is the same word used for Boaz and for the virtuous woman of Prov 31 (strength, worth, excellence — context determines which dimension is primary).
15: ad asher yaniyach YHWH la'acheikhem kakhem — "until the LORD gives your brothers rest (haniyach) as he has given you." The verb nwach (hiphil: give rest to) echoes through Joshua as the fulfillment vocabulary for the land-promises. Covenant solidarity means the Transjordanian tribes cannot enjoy their rest while their brothers are still in the wilderness.
16: kol asher titsavenu na'aseh vekhol asher tishlacheinu nelekh — "all that you command us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go." The people's response mirrors the unconditional obedience they pledged to Moses. The pairing of tsivah (command) and shalach (send) covers both instruction and commission — total availability to Joshua's authority.
17: raq yihyeh YHWH elohekha imakh ka'asher hayah im Moshe — "only may the LORD your God be with you as he was with Moses." The people's obedience is conditioned not on Joshua's own qualities but on his having what Moses had: the divine presence. This is perceptive theology: the continuity of authority is the continuity of the ehyeh imakh promise, not of the person's gifts.
18: kol ish asher yamreh et picha — "any man who rebels against your command." Marah = rebel, be rebellious/defiant — the same word used for Israel's wilderness rebellion. The death penalty for rebellion against Joshua's authority means that Joshua's command carries covenant weight — to reject him is to reject the YHWH-appointed leader. raq chazaq we'emats — the fourth repetition of the command, this time from the people to their leader: an act of encouragement and covenant commitment simultaneously.
